schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch date the sickness to the fourteenth year at the Assyrian invasion and reject the plague theory; Ellicott ties it to the anxiety of the peril and finds the recovery preceded the Assyrian catastrophe.

Matthew Henry meditates on mortality — neither greatness nor goodness exempts from death — reads the disease as the plague, and hears in 'set thine house in order' a call to set the heart in order too.
Clarke works the chronology (14 + 15 = 29 years) and reads 'set thine house in order' as making a will; Wesley notes that absolute threatenings often carry secret conditions.
Gill refers the sickness, the message, and the prayer to the fuller exposition elsewhere.

sides.position: the plague — the boil or plague-sore that also smote the Assyrians
not the plague; that theory loses its chief support, and the malady cannot be ascertained

themes: in the midst of life we are in death

schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads the turned face as privacy or a turning toward the temple, and weighs whether the tears were unwillingness to die or the importunity of lively prayer.

Ellicott compares the gesture in 1 Kings 21:4 and grief seeking a hiding-place; Keil and Delitzsch say Hezekiah wept because he was to die in the midst of life, when God had promised the righteous long life.
Wesley: he turned his face as he lay in bed, unable to reach his closet, but retired to God as well as he could.

themes: turning the face to the wall
dying in the midst of life

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch deny the plea is self-righteous, since a devoted heart is impossible without faith; Ellicott finds nothing surprising in it (cf. the Psalms, Neh. 13:14), reading it as deprecating an untimely death (Prov. 10:27).

Clarke judges the appeal to his own uprightness savoured little of humility, a thing winked at in the times of ignorance; Wesley reads 'in truth' as sincerity and the tears as the Old-Testament horror of death.
Matthew Henry: sincerity is pleaded not as a debt but as the condition of the covenant God himself wrought, Christ's righteousness alone being the purchase of mercy.

debates.question: Does Hezekiah's plea of his own integrity breathe humility or a want of it?

sides.position: a want of humility and self-knowledge, winked at under the old economy
not self-righteous trust but covenant sincerity, impossible without faith

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch stress the immediacy of the answer and read the disputed word as the central 'Zion city' where the citadel stood; Ellicott judges 'middle court' the reading of the versions, the Hebrew 'city' being wrong.
Clarke fixes the Keri/Ketiv (hatstser 'court' vs. ha'ir 'city'); Wesley takes it as the king's palace court, noting God's readiness to hear his children.
Gill lays out the choice between 'middle court' of the palace and 'middle city,' Jerusalem being in three parts.
Ellicott renders 'captain' as ruler/nagid, a title of honour (1 Sam. 10:1; 1 Kings 1:35) absent from the parallel; Keil and Delitzsch take the third-day assurance as a distinct pledge of recovery.
Matthew Henry finds a fresh mercy in every clause — spared for his people's sake and David's covenant, his prayer honoured and then exceeded with healing, the temple, fifteen years, and deliverance.
Gill glosses 'captain of my people' as the king of them, following the Targum.

themes: I have heard thy prayer, seen thy tears

schools.summary: Clarke holds that knowing one's death-date is no privilege but a misery; Wesley draws the matching lesson that God keeps us at uncertainties so we may always be ready.
Matthew Henry reads the fifteen years as a renewed lease and joins Wesley's moral that our ignorance of the day is designed to keep us watchful.
Ellicott reckons fourteen years and a fraction as fifteen and notes the deliverance clause shows the Assyrians had not yet retired (cf. 2 Kings 19:34).
Gill refers the fifteen years to the fuller treatment of the promise elsewhere.

outliers.note: Holds that foreknowing one's death-date is no privilege but a misery — 'a thousand deaths in fearing one' — from which God in mercy hides all other men.

themes: the mercy of not knowing our last day

schools.summary: Clarke identifies shechin as an inflammatory tumour of uncertain kind and grants the fig-poultice a proper medical use (cf. Pliny), yet only as the natural means God chose to bless.
Matthew Henry makes Isaiah the physician and draws two rules: use proper means when sick, else we tempt God; and despise not plain medicines, for the sudden cure was still miraculous.
Gill notes the dried figs were the less likely to cure, throwing the wonder into relief.
Keil and Delitzsch record simply that the lump of figs was laid on the boil and Hezekiah revived again.

Ellicott sets the sign beside 2 Kings 19:29 and Isaiah's offer to Ahaz (Isa. 7:11) as a token the word would come true; Keil and Delitzsch handle the grammar of the shadow-question over its perfect verb.
Clarke: Hezekiah sought one miracle to prove the other, that nothing might remain equivocal about a supernatural cure.
Matthew Henry contrasts Hezekiah with his father Ahaz, who refused a sign, and marks that the king desired recovery only to go up to the house of the LORD.
Gill notes Hezekiah asked the sign before the plaster of figs was laid on, his faith outrunning the remedy.

Ellicott suspects the Hebrew corrupt (statement or question?), shows 'degrees' means steps/stairs (Exod. 20:26; 1 Kings 10:20; Neh. 3:15), and follows Knobel that the dial of Ahaz was a column on a circular flight of steps; Keil confirms the crux.
Gill takes the plain sense: the sign assures the king of his cure and of going up to the temple on the third day.

themes: the make of the dial of Ahaz

schools.summary: Ellicott: the forward course seemed easy only because familiar, though it would be as wonderful; he reads 'go down' as 'spread.' Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to the LORD turning the shadow back on the gnomon of Ahaz.
Wesley weighs the motions: forward would be natural for the kind though miraculous for its swiftness, but backward is 'both ways miraculous.'
Matthew Henry: the king chose the backward sign as the more strange and the more significant of his returning to the days of his youth (Job 33:25).
Gill: forward was agreeable to the sun's course and so less apparent as a miracle; backward would be unmistakable.

themes: returning to the days of youth

schools.summary: Ellicott surveys the opinions (mock sun, vapour, earthquake, the fathers' receding sun, Keil's refraction, Thenius' eclipse-then-myth) and concludes the fact is certain divine agency but the manner undisclosed, the text saying 'shadow' not 'sun' (cf. Isa. 38:8).

Clarke argues for a God-wrought refraction of the light through dense vapours, not disturbing the heavens; Wesley takes the older view that the sun itself went back, and notes the Babylonians enquired of the miracle (2 Chron. 32:31).
Gill notes only that Isaiah prayed earnestly that Hezekiah's desire be granted.

debates.question: How was the shadow turned back ten degrees?

sides.position: by a God-wrought refraction of the sun's rays, not disturbing the heavens
the sun itself literally went back, and the shadow with it
the fact is certain divine agency, but the manner is left undisclosed

themes: a miracle at a prophet's prayer

schools.summary: Ellicott grounds the name in the cuneiform Marduk-abla-iddina and, with Keil and Delitzsch (following Josephus), reads the embassy's true aim as securing Hezekiah's resources and alliance against Assyria after Sennacherib's fall.
Clarke corrects Berodach to Merodach and notes the friendship and the report of the miracle; Wesley takes Berodach for a usurping viceroy seeking Hezekiah's help against Assyria.
Gill notes the name Merodach-baladan and refers the account to the parallel throughout.
Matthew Henry adds a religious motive beside the political — the sun-worshipping Babylonians honouring the favourite of the heaven whose sun went back for him.

outliers.note: Adds a religious motive beside the political: the sun-worshipping Babylonians did homage to Hezekiah as the favourite of the heaven whose sun had gone back for him.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read 'hearkened' as a copyist's error for 'was glad of them'; Ellicott raises the puzzle that the silver and gold seem to contradict the emptied treasury of 2 Kings 18:15, and reports the critics' rival datings.
Clarke: the king showed all through folly and exultation, a trial that proved his heart pride and folly; Wesley traces the display to his being lifted up by the honour done him (2 Chron. 32:25).
Matthew Henry calls him 'courteous to a fault,' faulting him for showing his treasures but not the temple or the law that might have won the envoys to the true religion.

debates.question: How can Hezekiah still have such treasures after emptying his coffers to Sennacherib (2 Kings 18:15-16)?

sides.position: the sickness and embassy fall before Sennacherib's invasion
he gave up only ready money and hid his real treasures, with fresh gifts flowing in afterward

themes: the contradiction with the emptied treasury

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read Isaiah's questions as meant to draw out the king's heart, his answer 'from a far country' betraying his vanity; Ellicott hears the prophet's fearless challenge and his settled opposition to foreign alliances (Isa. 30:1).
Matthew Henry marks the comforter turned reprover (John 16:7-8) and commends Hezekiah for submitting to the scrutiny without resentment.

themes: questions to draw out the heart

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch let the frank confession stand as the ground of the coming sentence — the vanity that hid nothing to be punished by the loss of all it displayed.
Matthew Henry commends the ingenuous confession yet faults the king for not bringing the envoys to Isaiah, the best treasure in his dominions, which might have prevented the false step.

themes: nothing that I have not shewed them

schools.summary: Ellicott presses the irony (friends turned robbers, allies turned conquerors) and holds the prophecy genuine supernatural prediction, positive in tone, against Thenius' naturalizing view.
Clarke fixes the fulfilment in the Babylonian captivity (Dan. 1:1-3); Wesley names the grounds — pride, ingratitude, and carnal confidence in the league with Babylon.
Matthew Henry draws the justice: God takes from us what we make the matter of our pride; the snake cherished will sting the bosom that warms it.

debates.question: Is Isaiah's word of the Babylonian exile genuine prediction or natural foresight?

sides.position: real supernatural prediction, positive and unconditional in tone
the shrewd political foresight of a statesman perceiving Judah's danger

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read 'sons' as descendants and 'eunuchs' rather as courtiers or chamberlains (cf. 1 Sam. 8:15), pointing to the fulfilment in Daniel and even Manasseh's captivity (2 Chron. 33:11).
Clarke likewise softens 'eunuchs' to household servants (cf. 2 Kings 24:13-15); Wesley keeps the horror — bodies enslaved and souls exposed to idolatry.

themes: royalty spent in a pagan palace

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take the reply as submission like Eli's (1 Sam. 3:18) and a candid acknowledgment of grace; Ellicott reads pious acquiescence and renders 'peace and permanence' (cf. Jer. 33:6).
Wesley reads only humble penitence, the king owning the sentence just and merciful; Clarke suspects an interested reply, careless of the coming calamity so his own days be peaceful.
Matthew Henry stands between, defending the confession that the word is good yet warning against being unconcerned for posterity.

debates.question: Is Hezekiah's 'peace and truth in my days' humble submission or self-interest?

sides.position: pious submission and penitence, humbled for his pride
an interested reply, careless of the calamity so his own days are peaceful
good so far as it goes, yet too unconcerned for posterity

themes: good is the word of the LORD
peace and truth in my days

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the basin and aqueduct bringing water into the city (cf. 2 Kings 18:17); Ellicott identifies the pool and the upper watercourse of Gihon (2 Chron. 32:30).
Clarke gathers Hezekiah's pride and riches from the Chronicles and reads the embassy as sowing the covetous seed of Babylon's later conquest, piling up secular parallels.
Gill refers 'all his might' to his wars and to the reformation of religion and abolition of idolatry.
Matthew Henry makes the water-conduit a great mercy — plenty at hand without striving or terror in the drawing.

outliers.note: Reads the embassy as the seed of Judah's ruin and piles up secular parallels — Goths and Vandals in Italy, Saxons and Danes in Britain — to show covetous conquest following displayed wealth.

themes: the rest of the acts of Hezekiah
pride as the seed of ruin
the good and the folly of the reign

schools.summary: Gill supplies from the Chronicles the honour of Hezekiah's burial in the chief of the sepulchres of David, with a great concourse, spices, and public mourning (2 Chron. 32:33).
Keil and Delitzsch record only the bare succession of Manasseh.
Matthew Henry finds the verse's weight in the untoward son — grace runs not in the blood — yet reads God's alternation of good and bad reigns as showing how loath he was to cut off his people.

themes: God loath to make an end
